---
title: "Further Reading"
description: "Curated resources for going deeper on Git, the terminal, research data management, and reproducible analysis."
---

Curated resources organized by topic. All free resources are marked with
the source — many are available online at no cost.

---

## Git Internals

**Pro Git** (Scott Chacon & Ben Straub)
The authoritative free book on Git, published by Apress and available at
[git-scm.com/book](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2). Chapters 1–3 cover everything
in this course. **Chapters 10–11** ("Git Internals" and "Git and Other Systems")
provide the deeper object model and plumbing command coverage referenced in
[Git Internals Deep Dive](git-internals-deep-dive.qmd).

**Git from the Bottom Up** (John Wiegley)
A 40-page essay explaining Git by starting with object storage and working up to
branches and merges — the inverse of how most tutorials teach it. Available free at
[jwiegley.github.io/git-from-the-bottom-up](https://jwiegley.github.io/git-from-the-bottom-up/).
Excellent complement to Module 01.

**GitLab Git Reference**
Concise command reference with examples, maintained by GitLab. Useful as a
secondary reference when GitHub's documentation is platform-specific.
→ [docs.gitlab.com/ee/topics/git](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/topics/git/)

---

## Terminal Skills

**The Linux Command Line** (William E. Shotts)
A comprehensive introduction to the Bash shell and Unix command-line tools,
free online at [linuxcommand.org/tlcl.php](https://linuxcommand.org/tlcl.php).
Chapters 1–8 cover everything in Module 00 and more. Part 4 covers shell scripting
(relevant to Module 11 hooks).

**Bash Reference Manual**
The official GNU documentation for Bash. Dense but complete — useful when you need
to understand shell syntax for writing hooks or aliases.
→ [gnu.org/software/bash/manual](https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/)

**Terminal Cheat Sheet** (SS64)
Quick reference for commands on macOS (`ss64.com/osx`), Linux Bash (`ss64.com/bash`),
and Windows CMD/PowerShell. Useful when looking up a specific command's flags.
→ [ss64.com](https://ss64.com)

---

## Research Data Management

**UK Data Service: Research Data Management**
Practical guidance for managing research data throughout the project lifecycle —
data management plans, file naming, storage, access, and sharing. Especially
relevant for clinical and health research.
→ [ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data](https://ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data/)

**FAIR Data Principles**
The FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) principles for scientific
data management. The original 2016 paper is freely available:
Wilkinson et al. (2016). *Scientific Data*, 3, 160018.
→ [doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18)

**Research Data Alliance (RDA): Active DMPs**
Working group resources on active data management plans that integrate version
control and provenance tracking. Useful context for why Git fits into a broader
data management strategy.
→ [rd-alliance.org](https://www.rd-alliance.org)

**OSF (Open Science Framework)**
A free platform for pre-registration, data sharing, and linking GitHub repositories
to publications. Widely used in rehabilitation and health science research.
→ [osf.io](https://osf.io)

---

## R Reproducibility

**renv documentation**
Official documentation for the `renv` R package for reproducible environments.
Covers the lock file format, snapshot/restore workflow, and integration with Git.
→ [rstudio.github.io/renv](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/)

**The Turing Way: Reproducible Research**
A community-maintained handbook covering version control, testing, continuous
integration, and reproducible environments — with R and Python examples.
Free online at [the-turing-way.netlify.app](https://the-turing-way.netlify.app).
Particularly relevant: "Version Control" and "Reproducible Environments" chapters.

**R for Reproducible Scientific Analysis** (Software Carpentry)
A workshop curriculum that teaches R, Git, and research best practices together.
Free at [swcarpentry.github.io/r-novice-gapminder](https://swcarpentry.github.io/r-novice-gapminder/).
Useful companion to this course for learners who are new to R.

---

## Python Reproducibility

**Python Packaging User Guide**
Official guide to `pip`, virtual environments, and `requirements.txt` — the Python
equivalent of `renv.lock`. Essential for Python-based research projects.
→ [packaging.python.org](https://packaging.python.org)

**nbstripout documentation**
Tool for clearing Jupyter notebook outputs before committing — covered briefly
in Module 11. The README explains the Git filter mechanism in detail.
→ [github.com/kynan/nbstripout](https://github.com/kynan/nbstripout)

**Jupytext documentation**
Alternative approach: sync `.ipynb` files to plain `.py` or `.Rmd` files for
readable diffs in Git. Complements nbstripout for collaborative notebook workflows.
→ [jupytext.readthedocs.io](https://jupytext.readthedocs.io)

---

## Rehabilitation Research Workflows

**Stroke rehabilitation analysis on GitHub** (example workflows)
Several published rehabilitation studies include their analysis code on GitHub.
Searching "stroke rehabilitation analysis R GitHub" or "physiotherapy outcome measure
reproducible analysis" surfaces real examples of the patterns taught in this course.
The `rehab-research` example repository used throughout this course follows
these real-world conventions.

**CONSORT statement and data transparency**
The CONSORT 2010 statement encourages transparency in reporting randomized trials.
Linking a GitHub repository (tagged at the analysis version used for submission)
to a trial registration is increasingly expected by high-impact journals.
→ [consort-statement.org](http://www.consort-statement.org)

**EQUATOR Network: Reporting Guidelines**
A library of reporting guidelines for health research (CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, etc.).
Each guideline increasingly expects or requires data and code availability — Git
provides the version control infrastructure.
→ [equator-network.org](https://www.equator-network.org)
